steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the main idea of the text. The passage praises the book's ambition and scope, then pivots with "But" to identify a shortcoming: the absence of a unifying authorial point of view. The main idea must capture both the genuine value and this structural limitation.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — The compilers of The Fifty-Year Mission had lofty goals for their oral history of the Star Trek franchise, but the published work lacks information about many key events in the franchise's history.: The text's criticism is about the lack of an authorial voice, not about missing events. The passage explicitly says the book includes accounts from every series and film ever made. ✗
  • Choice B — The Fifty-Year Mission includes more accounts from people involved with Star Trek television shows than it does from people involved with Star Trek films.: The text never compares the ratio of TV versus film accounts; it states the book covers both. This detail is invented. ✗
  • Choice C — The large amount of material compiled into The Fifty-Year Mission is surprising given that many of the people involved in the Star Trek franchise did not participate in the oral history project.: The text implies broad participation by noting accounts from cast and crew of every series and film — it never suggests many people declined to participate. ✗
  • Choice D — The Fifty-Year Mission represents a worthwhile attempt to thoroughly recount the history of the Star Trek franchise, but its approach has an important limitation.: Captures both halves of the passage — the genuine value ("unique and valuable resource") and the structural flaw (no authorial voice to unify accounts). ✓
That matches Choice D — The Fifty-Year Mission represents a worthwhile attempt to thoroughly recount the history of the Star Trek franchise, but its approach has an important limitation..
